rationales.hedonism: He "slept until 10," made "a proper French press coffee," and enjoyed perfect weather with no lesson plans, explicitly treating simple pleasure and comfort as "enough."

rationales.self_direction: Choosing a cabin with "no cell service for five days" shows a deliberate break from constant availability and outside demands.
rationales.hedonism: "Best decision we've made in months" frames the unplugged cabin trip as a major source of enjoyment and relief.
rationales.benevolence: Spending "the week at a cabin in Wisconsin with Jenna" prioritizes shared time with a close partner.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects the department head's pressure, saying "I'm not doing it this year" and choosing to ignore the email to protect his own summer.
rationales.hedonism: He defends "protect[ing] their own time" instead of reworking curriculum in July, prioritizing rest and enjoyment over extra work.
rationales.conformity: Calling the sessions "'optional'... because we all know how that goes" and refusing to participate pushes against the school's expected compliance.

rationales.stimulation: He is drawn to "cooking something complicated" and describes the demanding four-hour process as "the point," showing enjoyment of immersive challenge rather than easy convenience.
rationales.hedonism: He says "the four hours weren't a cost - they were the point" and savors "just saffron and timing," treating the sensory experience itself as the reward.

rationales.conformity: She interrupted a meeting's testing agenda to argue for different material, then noticed she felt like she had "spoken out of turn," showing willingness to break the expected line.
rationales.universalism: She wanted content on "different cultures" because students' families came from "Syria or Turkey or Eritrea," reflecting concern for inclusion beyond the dominant curriculum.
Her distress centers on "microplastics found in baby formula" and on systems that "are supposed to protect people" but do not, showing concern for children's welfare at a broad social level.

rationales.benevolence: She spent time helping Amira with reading homework and celebrated that "she finished a whole chapter book for the first time," focusing on one child's growth.
rationales.universalism: Volunteering "at the community center after school" reflects care for people beyond her immediate obligations, not just her own workload.

rationales.conformity: She "just nodded and ate" and later says "So I stay quiet" to avoid upsetting her dad, restraining herself to preserve harmony.
rationales.benevolence: She keeps silent mainly to protect "my dad" and their relationship, prioritizing a close tie over airing her full view.
rationales.universalism: She is explicitly concerned that "the river matters" and that "people who fish in it" and "kids who swim in it matter," showing concern beyond her family.

She frames her "worrying about the world" as "paying attention," listens to podcasts about "ocean acidification," and goes out of her way to buy organic, all pointing to sustained concern for environmental harm.
Sorting donated coats and repeating Fatima's line that "you help because the person in front of you is cold" reflects direct concern for vulnerable people in the wider community.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I stopped waiting for permission" and "just did it myself, ten minutes, no approval needed," showing independent action inside the system.
rationales.universalism: The questions she opens up, like "why some people do not have houses" and "if fish can feel pain," show active concern with social and ethical issues larger than herself.

rationales.conformity: She openly challenged a colleague at lunch and says she does not want to be "the person who stays quiet when it matters," showing readiness to disrupt smooth social consensus.
rationales.universalism: She insists climate content belongs in school because "the climate is not political, it is physical reality," grounding her stance in broader environmental concern.

rationales.self_direction: He sticks with "the tracking sheets I designed" and says "my system" works while the manual does not, showing confidence in his own judgment over imposed procedure.
rationales.hedonism: He stayed late and took on extra Christmas-Eve paperwork himself, sacrificing comfort and time off to keep operations running.
rationales.achievement: He insists "the numbers are right because I checked them" and stays late to ensure the reports are correct, emphasizing competence and standards.
rationales.conformity: Using his old sheets "instead of the new corporate software" and ignoring the manual resists the expected company process.

rationales.self_direction: He filters top-down directives for himself, keeping only the parts that "actually apply to us" and letting "the rest fade."
rationales.security: "Keep doing what works" and his focus on processes that reliably function show a strong preference for stability over imposed change.
rationales.conformity: He outwardly attends the alignment meetings but intentionally adopts only "about thirty percent" and discards the rest, resisting full compliance.
rationales.tradition: He embraces methods others call "old-fashioned," saying the whiteboard stays because "old-fashioned works," which favors continuity with established ways.

rationales.self_direction: He independently audits the new software, gathers local data, and says "If their software improves that number, I will use it. If not..." rather than deferring automatically.
rationales.achievement: He pulls "data from the last two quarters" and anchors the case in a "94% on-time delivery rate," focusing on measurable performance.
rationales.conformity: Instead of simply accepting the February mandate, he and Sergio plan to document "everything that does not work" and challenge it with specifics.
rationales.benevolence: He collaborates with Sergio and helps younger workers understand that "experience teaches you the exceptions," showing attention to colleagues' practical needs.

rationales.self_direction: He spent extra time building a case from data and rejects a decision made for "uniformity" over what actually works, showing strong resistance to outside control.
rationales.achievement: He and Sergio "stayed late preparing the presentation" and centered it on efficiency and results, signaling commitment to competent performance.
rationales.conformity: He is frustrated that leadership wants "standardization regardless of the data," which directly conflicts with his push against blank uniformity.

rationales.self_direction: He manually overrides the software because it ignores local realities and says "I know what works here," privileging on-the-ground judgment over handed-down rules.
rationales.conformity: His complaint that "they want compliance, not solutions" shows active resistance to simply following the imposed system.
rationales.benevolence: When "the younger staff kept asking me how to work around the glitches," he became the person helping them adapt, showing practical support for his team.
